Part B.key_evidence: Single detection (ndethist=1, deltajd=0) at a position covered 699 times previously, indicating a brand-new source at a long-monitored location. Positive subtraction with magpsf~17.8 in g. The nearest PS1 source is 14.9 arcsec away (distpsnr1), so there is no catalog host at the candidate position; the bright object visible in the reference/science is offset from center. Difference cutout shows a localized positive residual at center with no counterpart in the reference. classtar=0.038 is low but fwhm=6.44 pix is somewhat broad.
Part B.leading_interpretation_and_support: Most consistent with an asteroid: an isolated, first-time, point-like positive residual with no host galaxy or PS1 counterpart within arcseconds, despite the field being imaged hundreds of times. A moving solar-system object naturally explains a one-off detection at a well-covered location with nothing in the reference.
Part B.alternative_analysis: A hostless or faint-host supernova is possible—ZTF can detect SNe with no cataloged PS1 host if the host is below PS1 depth. However, the complete absence of any reference flux at center and the single-epoch nature without subsequent detections fit an asteroid more cleanly than a SN, which would typically persist and show an underlying host. AGN/variable star are disfavored given ndethist=1 and no stellar/nuclear counterpart at center.
